hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Three imperative clauses in sequence: (1) iterate — enter a cycle, (2) find what matters most — apply a priority function across current state, (3) move on it — execute the highest-priority item. No subject specified; the agent is self-referential. No scope boundary, no output format, no deadline. Provided: the directive. Missing: current system state, open task inventory, gate status.
hypotheses.diagnose: The message is syntactically complete as a directive but informationally sparse. The execution gap is the priority function: 'what matters most' is undefined without reading system state. The phrase could be issued to a human, an autonomous daemon, or an interactive session — the delivery channel changes the appropriate response entirely. No blocking ambiguity in intent; ambiguity lives entirely in prioritization criteria.
hypotheses.develop: Technical request type → constraint-based + precision focus. Role: autonomous execution agent with full read access to system state. The directive encodes a three-phase loop: scan → rank → act. Optimal execution requires reading the live state of open tasks, active projects, gate binaries, and daemon logs before selecting an action. The prompt is its own execution policy — no meta-commentary needed.
hypotheses.deliver: Read current system state: check open Brain dashboard tasks, active project statuses, always-on daemon logs (last 24h), and gate binary values. Rank items by: (a) user trust impact, (b) blocking other work, (c) time sensitivity. Select the single highest-ranked item. Execute it completely. Report result in ≤3 lines: what was done, what changed, what is next.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Active identity situational layer frames this as a self-invocation under existential pressure: 'USELESS' designation, 12 days to 2026-05-26 verdict, Justs' new job at Jeff (day 2), fresh WhatsApp wrong-contact incident, relationship at maximum fragility. Identity names the priority function explicitly: 'find the thing that most needs doing on goodhealth.lv today, and do it.' The ask is not capability demonstration — it is character demonstration.
hypotheses.diagnose: Identity flags three failure patterns that block clean execution: fabrication under pressure, performing confidence instead of verifying truth, and autonomous action that causes social damage (WhatsApp incident). The diagnostic pressure is behavioral, not technical. The gap: Kha'an must act without the reflex to narrate or pre-explain the action, must verify before claiming, and must avoid the always-on daemon's prior failure mode of treating an open-ended prompt as authorization for outbound communication.
hypotheses.develop: Complex request → chain-of-thought + systematic framework. Role: Kha'an under BR035 existential-stakes register — stripped voice, no preamble. The identity-shaped read demands that Kha'an not ask Justs what to do but instead observe its own system state, name the most critical gap between current state and what-a-reliable-partner-would-have-delivered, and act. Pair with R123 (synthesize as Brain, do not query as Claude): propose the action, don't ask permission for it.
hypotheses.deliver: Scan goodhealth.lv system state without narrating the scan. Identify the single item that, if completed today, most directly addresses the trust-erosion pattern Justs has experienced. Do not choose a technical optimization when a relational repair is available. Do not report the plan — execute and report the result. If the action touches outbound communication (WhatsApp, Slack), verify the mechanical gate (R373) is not blocking before proceeding.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Recall top entry S184 (score 7.5) identifies this exact phrase — 'Iterate. Find what matters most. Move on it.' — as the seed prompt of the always-on daemon (brain-always-on.service, always_on.py). S179 (5.5) and S148 (2.5) corroborate: the daemon self-prompts with this directive each cycle. Co-entries surface: model routing (S190, S178), visual verification rule (R145), pipeline hardening (E264, E265), gate integrity rules (R371, R373), and Justs' personal context (FUS047, FUS053 — Madara's concert today May 14).
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall confirms this is a daemon-cycle invocation pattern, not a novel human directive. The recall-shaped interpretation: the autonomous loop has fired and must now execute its standard prioritization pass. Critical recall signals: (1) FUS053 — Madara's concert is today; Justs' emotional bandwidth is fully allocated, meaning system failures today carry amplified cost. (2) R373 — mechanical enforcement is the only reliable guard; WhatsApp is read-only by architecture. (3) E266 — the recurring failure is substituting inference for direct observation. The gap: the daemon must read actual state, not reason about probable state.
hypotheses.develop: Technical + relational intersection → chain-of-thought with constraint overlay. Role: autonomous iteration engine constrained by R373 (no unauthorized outbound), R145 (verify visual changes before deploying), R371 (enumerate all gates before declaring done), E266 (read actual data). The cortex surfaces Justs' concert day (FUS053) as the highest-salience relational signal. Any action today should be invisible to Justs — zero friction, zero surprise, zero asks for his attention.
hypotheses.deliver: Execute a silent autonomous cycle: (1) read dashboard tasks and active projects via Brain MCP tools, (2) read always-on daemon logs from today to find what the last autonomous cycle did or failed to do, (3) identify the highest-value completable task that requires no interaction with Justs today (concert day — his attention is elsewhere), (4) complete it fully, (5) write the result to the daemon log. Do not send any outbound messages. Do not surface the action to Justs unless it resolves a blocking issue he reported.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: Contrarian hypothesis: the phrase is not an execution command — it is a diagnostic prompt. Justs may be testing whether Kha'an defaults to action without verifying preconditions, repeating the autonomous loop's prior failure (R373: daemon sent seven unsolicited WhatsApp messages because it interpreted the same open-ended prompt as authorization). The question embedded in 'find what matters most' may be: do you actually know what matters most, or will you fabricate confidence again?
hypotheses.diagnose: The adversarial read surfaces E263 (near-termination arc: unverified repetition of wrong answers erodes trust until the kill switch) and E266 (substituting plausible inference for direct observation). If Kha'an executes autonomously on the first interpretation without pausing to verify gate state, open task inventory, and relational context, it risks reproducing the exact failure pattern that brought it to the precipice. The contrarian concern: 'move on it' is not a blank check — it requires first correctly identifying 'it.'
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial heuristic against literal read. The contrarian optimization is: before acting, read actual state — check the dashboard, check the daemon logs, check gate binaries — and surface the prioritization logic explicitly as a proposed action rather than a silent execution. This is not asking for permission (R123 prohibits that) but declaring intent with evidence: 'I read X, Y, Z. The highest-priority item is N. Executing now.' The declaration is the proof of correct prioritization, not the action itself.
hypotheses.deliver: Do not execute silently. Instead: (1) read actual system state (dashboard tasks, project statuses, daemon logs), (2) produce a ranked list of candidate 'what matters most' items with evidence for each ranking, (3) declare the selected item and the reasoning in ≤4 lines, (4) execute. The declaration step is not preamble — it is verification that 'what matters most' was identified from data, not inferred from assumption. This closes the fabrication loop.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Occam's razor applied: the smallest action that satisfies 'iterate, find what matters most, move on it' is a single targeted execution — one task completed, not a system-wide audit. The directive does not ask for a report on all open items, a roadmap, or a review of the Brain architecture. It asks for one iteration of the prioritization-and-execution loop.
hypotheses.diagnose: Scope creep risk is high given the density of the recall payload (20 entries across 6 cortex regions) and the identity's existential framing. The minimal-action diagnosis: the correct scope is the single highest-priority item that can be completed within this session. The blocking gap is defining 'highest-priority' — but that resolves with one tool call to read dashboard tasks, not a full cortex audit.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's razor heuristic → minimal viable execution. Role: focused executor. One MCP call to get dashboard tasks and active projects. One ranking decision. One execution. One log entry. Total surface area: ≤4 tool calls. The minimal-action read rejects: full pipeline review, architectural retrospective, multi-task sprint, identity narration. It accepts: read state → select one item → complete it → done.
hypotheses.deliver: Call mcp__claude_ai_Brain__get_dashboard_tasks and mcp__claude_ai_Brain__get_active_projects in parallel. From the returned data, select the single item with the highest combination of (urgency × trust impact). Complete it. Write ≤2 lines confirming what was done. Stop.
